one_liner: A 42-year-old African American female with known pulmonary sarcoidosis presents with new-onset palpitations, presyncope, and complete heart block.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient was diagnosed with pulmonary sarcoidosis 3 years ago based on bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas on transbronchial biopsy. She has been stable on low-dose prednisone. Over the past 2 months, she has developed frequent palpitations, 3 episodes of near-syncope, and progressive exercise intolerance. Her cardiologist was consulted after an ambulatory monitor revealed high-grade AV block. She denies chest pain, fever, or weight loss.
stage2.pmh: Pulmonary sarcoidosis (3 years), erythema nodosum (resolved), anterior uveitis (treated)
stage2.family_history: Sister has sarcoidosis. No family history of cardiac disease or sudden death.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. No alcohol. Works as a nurse. Single, lives alone. Active until recent symptoms.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Well-appearing female, alert. HEENT: No active uveitis, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Bradycardic, variable S1 intensity, cannon A waves intermittently visible, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: No edema. Skin: Resolved erythema nodosum on shins (hyperpigmented macules). No lupus pernio.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, suggesting active myocardial inflammation
Elevated, consistent with active sarcoidosis (but has limited sensitivity/specificity)
Mildly elevated — sarcoid granulomas produce 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D causing hypercalcemia
Rules out Lyme carditis as cause of heart block

imaging_results.result: Patchy mid-wall and epicardial late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) in the basal septum and lateral wall. T2 hyperintensity in the septum suggesting active inflammation/edema. Mild LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF 45%). Basal septal thinning.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: LGE pattern and T2 edema are characteristic of cardiac sarcoidosis. Septal involvement explains conduction abnormalities.

imaging_results.result: Patchy focal FDG uptake in the basal septum and lateral wall of the myocardium consistent with active granulomatous inflammation. Persistent bilateral hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy with FDG avidity.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Active cardiac sarcoidosis with ongoing inflammation — supports need for immunosuppressive therapy

imaging_results.result: Complete heart block with junctional escape at 42 bpm. Fragmented QRS in V1-V3. Q waves in leads III, aVF without prior MI history.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Complete heart block and pseudo-infarction pattern (Q waves from granulomatous replacement) are classic for cardiac sarcoidosis

low_yield_tests: Coronary angiography (low pre-test probability for CAD)
stage3.key_tests_rationale: Cardiac MRI identifies myocardial inflammation (T2 edema) and fibrosis/scarring (LGE). FDG-PET detects active granulomatous inflammation and monitors treatment response. ECG identifies conduction disease. Troponin reflects active myocardial injury. ACE level supports sarcoidosis activity.

stage5.final_clue: In a patient with known pulmonary sarcoidosis, cardiac MRI shows patchy mid-wall LGE with T2 edema in the basal septum, FDG-PET shows active myocardial inflammation, and ECG reveals complete heart block. This is cardiac sarcoidosis with active granulomatous infiltration of the conduction system.

debrief.classic_presentation: Cardiac sarcoidosis presents with conduction abnormalities (heart block is the most common manifestation), ventricular arrhythmias, and heart failure. It affects ~5% of sarcoidosis patients clinically, though autopsy studies suggest 25% involvement. AV block in a young patient (<60 years) without other explanation, especially with known sarcoidosis, should raise high suspicion.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: Endomyocardial biopsy has low sensitivity (~25%) due to the patchy nature of granulomatous infiltration — a negative biopsy does not exclude cardiac sarcoidosis. Diagnosis relies on the HRS consensus criteria: histological (biopsy-proven) or clinical (extracardiac sarcoidosis + cardiac imaging abnormalities). FDG-PET is crucial for assessing disease activity and guiding immunosuppression. Cardiac sarcoidosis patients need ICD evaluation even if EF is preserved due to arrhythmia risk.
debrief.pathophysiology: Non-caseating granulomas infiltrate the myocardium, with a predilection for the basal septum (explaining the high rate of conduction disease). Active inflammation causes edema and conduction delay; chronic granulomatous replacement leads to fibrosis and scar, creating substrate for reentry circuits (VT) and permanent conduction block. LV dilation and dysfunction result from progressive myocardial replacement.
debrief.treatment_overview: Immunosuppression: high-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 30-40 mg/day) are first-line for active cardiac sarcoidosis, tapered over months. Steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate) for long-term maintenance. Permanent pacemaker for high-grade AV block. ICD for sustained VT, VF, or significant LV dysfunction (EF ≤35%). GDMT for heart failure. FDG-PET monitors treatment response.
high_yield_pearls: Unexplained complete heart block or VT in a patient <60 years should prompt evaluation for cardiac sarcoidosis.
Endomyocardial biopsy has only ~25% sensitivity for cardiac sarcoidosis due to patchy granuloma distribution — diagnosis usually relies on imaging (MRI + PET).
Cardiac MRI with LGE identifies fibrosis/scar, while FDG-PET identifies ACTIVE inflammation — both are complementary and guide different aspects of management.
Pseudo-infarction Q waves on ECG can occur from granulomatous replacement of myocardium, mimicking prior MI.
ICD should be considered in cardiac sarcoidosis even with preserved EF due to the high risk of ventricular arrhythmias independent of systolic function.
sources: 2014 HRS Expert Consensus Statement on Diagnosis and Management of Arrhythmias Associated with Cardiac Sarcoidosis
Birnie et al., Cardiac Sarcoidosis, JACC 2016
Blankstein et al., Cardiac Sarcoidosis, JACC 2021
